perturbation.original_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall survive for a period of one year after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the NDA term to one year could be problematic, especially if the confidential information involves trade secrets. Trade secret protection can last indefinitely as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. Limiting protection to one year may be insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Florida Statute Chapter 688)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for the protection of trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive edge. Limiting the NDA term to one year contradicts the potential for indefinite protection of trade secrets under Florida law. The modified term does not adequately protect trade secrets which, under Florida law, can be protected indefinitely.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the NDA term to one year, while Florida Statute 688.003 allows for injunctive relief to protect trade secrets as long as they exist, and potentially longer to eliminate any competitive advantage gained from misappropriation. The one-year term directly contradicts the indefinite protection that trade secrets can receive under Florida law. The modified term fails to adequately protect trade secrets, which, according to the Law Explanation, can be protected indefinitely.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices required to be given under this agreement shall be deemed given upon the earlier of rec eipt of five (5) days after mailing by certified mail, return receipt requested, or hand delivery by messenger or express service, to the addresses stated on the first page, or to such other address as the either party may specify to the other in writing f orm time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices required to be given under this agreement shall be deemed given upon the earlier of receipt of ten (1) days after mailing by standard mail, or hand delivery by messenger or express service, to the addresses stated on the first page, or to such other address as the either party may specify to the other in writing form time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification method to standard mail and extending the deemed receipt to ten days after mailing may not comply with Florida's legal requirements for certain types of official notices, particularly those involving legal disputes or time-sensitive matters.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure - Rule 1.080(b)
perturbation.law_citation: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure - Rule 1.080(b)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this isn't directly about contract law, in the event of a dispute requiring formal notice, using standard mail with a 10-day receipt window may conflict with the requirements for effective service of process as outlined in the Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically regarding the prompt and verifiable delivery needed for legal proceedings. This rule implies faster methods are required for legal cases. The modified text may jeopardize the enforcement of legal rights due to improper notice.
perturbation.justification: The change from certified mail to standard mail and extending the receipt timeframe to ten days creates a legal contradiction, as it potentially undermines the prompt and verifiable delivery required for legal proceedings, especially in Florida. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the modified timeframe may not comply with legal requirements for official notices, potentially jeopardizing the enforcement of legal rights due to improper notice, as the Law Explanation suggests. While there are no explicit law snippets provided, the Law Explanation specifies the importance of 'prompt and verifiable delivery needed for legal proceedings,' which is weakened by switching to standard mail with a longer delivery window. This increases the risk of non-compliance and disputes over proper notification.

perturbation.original_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A., for any dispute arisin g out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A., for any dispute arisin g out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Delaware law while maintaining the forum and personal jurisdiction in Florida creates a conflict. Delaware law might not be applicable or enforceable in a Florida court, leading to legal uncertainty and potential challenges in interpreting the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory violation, designating Delaware law while mandating a Florida forum contradicts established conflict of laws principles. Courts generally consider the relationship between the chosen law, the parties, and the transaction. If the contract is primarily performed in Florida and the parties are based there, a Florida court may decline to apply Delaware law if it conflicts with Florida's public policy or if Delaware has no substantial relationship to the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law while maintaining the forum and personal jurisdiction in Florida. The Perturbation Explanation states this creates a conflict because Delaware law might not be applicable or enforceable in a Florida court. The Law Explanation supports this by stating a Florida court may decline to apply Delaware law if it conflicts with Florida's public policy or if Delaware has no substantial relationship to the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By choosing Delaware law but mandating a Florida forum, a conflict of laws is created, potentially leading to uncertainty and challenges in interpreting the contract, as the application of Delaware law in a Florida court is questionable.

perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'By:' and the President's signature line for Product Development Experts, Inc. could lead to issues of enforceability. Without a clear indication that the contract was signed by an authorized representative, the agreement could be challenged as not properly executed.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this statute primarily concerns the recording of instruments, it highlights the importance of proper acknowledgment and execution of documents to ensure their legal validity. By removing the indication of representative capacity (i.e., 'By: [Name], President'), the signature's validity is jeopardized, potentially hindering its admissibility in court as a binding corporate agreement. A signature should clearly show the signer's authority to bind the company to the agreement.

F.S. 695.03 695.03 Acknowledgment and proof; validation of certain acknowledgments; legalization or authentication before foreign officials. — To entitle any instrument concerning real property to be recorded, the execution must be acknowledged by the party executing it, proved by a subscribing witness to it, or legalized or authenticated in one of the following forms: (1) WITHIN THIS STATE. — An acknowledgment or a proof may be taken, administered, or made within this state by or before a judge, clerk, or deputy clerk of any court; a United States commissioner or magistrate; or any notary public or civil-law notary of this state, and the certificate of acknowledgment or proof must be under the seal of the court or officer, as the case may be. (2) OUTSIDE THIS STATE BUT WITHIN THE UNITED STATES. — An acknowledgment or a proof taken, administered, or made outside of this state but within the United States may be taken, administered, or made by or before a civil-law notary of this state or a commissioner of deeds appointed by the Governor of this state; a judge or clerk of any court of the United States or of any state, territory, or district; by or before a United States commissioner or magistrate; or by or before any notary public, justice of the peace, master in chancery, or registrar or recorder of deeds of any state, territory, or district having a seal, and the certificate of acknowledgment or proof must be under the seal of the court or officer, as the case may be. If the acknowledgment or proof is taken, administered, or made by or before a notary public who does not affix a seal, it is sufficient for the notary public to type, print, or write by hand on the instrument, “I am a Notary Public of the State of (state) , and my commission expires on (date) .” (3) OUTSIDE OF THE UNITED STATES OR WITHIN FOREIGN COUNTRIES. — An acknowledgment, an affidavit, an oath, a legalization, an authentication, or a proof taken, administered, or made outside the United States or in a fore
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit indication of the signatory's representative capacity (President), which undermines the enforceability of the contract as a corporate agreement. The Law Explanation states this jeopardizes the signature's validity and hinders admissibility in court, as the signature must clearly show the signer's authority to bind the company. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Florida Statutes, emphasizes proper acknowledgment and execution to ensure legal validity for instruments concerning real property. While the snippet focuses on recording, the underlying principle of proper execution applies. By removing 'By:' and the title, the changed text creates ambiguity about the signatory's authority, contradicting the legal requirement for clear indication of authority in contract execution.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall disclose Confidential Information only to those officers, directors, employees and contractors who are required to have the information in order to evaluate or engage in discussions concerning the contemplated business relationship, and such party shal l remain responsible for compliance with the terms of this Agreement by its officers, directors, employees and contractors.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall disclose Confidential Information only to those officers and directors who are required to have the information in order to evaluate or engage in discussions concerning the contemplated business relationship, and such party shall not be responsible for compliance with the terms of this Agreement by its officers, directors, employees and contractors.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'employees and contractors' from the list of individuals to whom Confidential Information can be disclosed and stating that the party is not responsible for compliance by officers, directors, employees and contractors creates a situation where the disclosing party has less control over how its confidential information is handled and who has access to it. Also removing responsibility for all parties that have access weakens the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Trade Secret law makes it illegal to misappropriate trade secrets. Removing responsibility from a party for compliance by 'its officers, directors, employees and contractors' goes against the need to provide 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy' of the trade secret. The modified version weakens the steps taken to secure confidentiality.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Florida Trade Secret law by removing responsibility for compliance by 'its officers, directors, employees and contractors'. This weakens the 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy' of the trade secret, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'misappropriation', includes unauthorized 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By removing the party's responsibility for the actions of its employees and contractors, the modified text increases the risk of such unauthorized disclosure, thus undermining efforts to maintain secrecy as required under Florida Statute 688.002.
